---
title: "DBSCAN Clustering"
format: html
---

## Overview

DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) discovers clusters of arbitrary shape based on point density. Unlike K-Means, it does not require specifying the number of clusters in advance and can identify noise points that don't belong to any cluster.

## Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Epsilon | 1.0 | Neighborhood radius — the maximum distance between two points to be considered neighbors |
| Min Points | 5 | Minimum number of points required to form a dense region (core point threshold) |

## When to Use

- You don't know the number of clusters
- Clusters have arbitrary shapes (non-spherical)
- Your data contains noise or outliers that should not be assigned to any cluster
- Cluster densities are roughly uniform

## Noise Detection

DBSCAN labels points that are not within `epsilon` distance of at least `min_points` other points as **noise**. These points are not assigned to any cluster. The cluster output panel shows the noise point count.

## Output

DBSCAN produces hard cluster assignments plus noise detection:

- **Entity groups**: One putative group per discovered cluster
- **Interval series**: Contiguous runs of each cluster assignment
- **Noise points**: Reported separately, not assigned to any group

## Tips

- **Epsilon** is the most important parameter. If all points are noise, increase epsilon. If everything is one cluster, decrease it.
- Enable z-score normalization so that epsilon has consistent meaning across features with different scales
- **Min Points** controls sensitivity: higher values create fewer, larger clusters
- Start with `min_points = 2 × num_features` as a rule of thumb
